perturbation.original_text: The undersigned acknowledge and agree that the foregoing statement on this Schedule 13G is filed on behalf of each of the undersigned and that all subsequent amendments to this statement on Schedule 13G shall be filed on behalf of each of the undersigned without the necessity of filing additional joint acquisition statements.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned acknowledge and agree that the foregoing statement on this Schedule 13G is filed on behalf of each of the undersigned and that all subsequent amendments to this statement on Schedule 13G shall be filed on behalf of only the primary filer; other filers have no further obligations.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the obligation of filing subsequent amendments removes the joint responsibility, potentially conflicting with SEC regulations regarding continuous disclosure by all parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 13d-2 requires amendments to Schedule 13G filings to be filed promptly when material changes occur. This modification suggests only the primary filer is responsible, contradicting the requirement for all signatories to ensure continuous and accurate disclosure.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: this section, file an amendment on Schedule 13G ( § 240.13d-102 ) within five business days after the end of the first month in which the person's direct or indirect beneficial ownership, computed as of the last day of the month, increases or decreases by more than five percent of the class of equity securities. Once an amendment has been filed reflecting beneficial ownership of five percent or less of the class of securities, no additional filings are required by this paragraph (c) . ( d ) Any person relying on § 240.13d-1(c) that has filed its initial Schedule 13G ( § 240.13d-102 ) pursuant to § 240.13d-1(c) shall, in addition to filing any amendments pursuant to paragraph (b) of this section, file an amendment on Schedule 13G ( § 240.13d-102 ) within two business days after acquiring, directly or indirectly, greater than 10 percent of a class of equity securities specified in § 240.13d-1(d) , and thereafter within two business days after increasing or decreasing its beneficial ownership by more than five percent of the class of equity securities. Once an amendment has been filed reflecting beneficial ownership of five percent or less of the class of securities, no additional filings are required by this paragraph (d) . ( e ) The first electronic amendment to a paper format Schedule 13D ( § 240.13d-101 of this chapter ) or Schedule 13G ( § 240.13d-102 of this chapter ) shall restate the entire text of the Schedule 13D or 13G, but previously filed paper exhibits to such Schedules are not required to be restated electronically. See Rule 102 of Regulation S-T ( § 232.102 of this chapter ) regarding amendments to exhibits previously filed in paper format. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if the sole purpose of filing the first electronic Schedule 13D or 13G amendment is to report a change in beneficial ownership that would terminate the filer's obligation to report, the amendment need not include a restatement of the entire text of the Schedule being amended. Note to § 240.13 d -2: For persons filing a short-form statement pursuant to Rule 13d-1(b) or (c), see also Rules 13d-1(e), (f), and (g). perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the requirement for all signatories to ensure continuous and accurate disclosure in Schedule 13G filings. The original text states that all subsequent amendments to Schedule 13G shall be filed on behalf of each of the undersigned, while the changed text limits this obligation to only the primary filer. According to the scraped law snippets, specifically Rule 13d-2, amendments to Schedule 13G must be filed promptly when material changes occur. By removing the obligation for all filers except the primary filer to file amendments, the changed text undermines the purpose of the rule, which is to ensure continuous and accurate disclosure by all relevant parties.

perturbation.original_text: The undersigned acknowledge that each shall be responsible for the timely filing of such amendments and for the completeness and accuracy of the information concerning him or it contained herein or therein, but shall not be responsible for the completeness and accuracy of the information concerning the others, except to the extent that he or it knows or has reason to believe that such information is inaccurate.
perturbation.changed_text: The undersigned acknowledge that they are only responsible for the information they directly provide, and have no responsibility to ensure the accuracy of any other information contained within the filing, even if they know it is inaccurate.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the responsibility clause such that the parties are not responsible even if they have knowledge of an inaccuracy removes the 'good faith' aspect of filing, contradicting securities law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 18(a) imposes liability for false or misleading statements in filed documents. By stating that there is no responsibility even with the knowledge of inaccuracies, this directly contradicts the liability imposed by the Securities Exchange Act.

Liability for misleading statements (a) Persons liable; persons entitled to recover; defense of good faith; suit at law or in equity; costs, etc. Any person who shall make or cause to be made any statement in any application, report, or document filed pursuant to this chapter or any rule or regulation thereunder or any undertaking contained in a registration statement as provided in subsection (d) of section 78o of this title , which statement
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes responsibility for accuracy even with knowledge of inaccuracies, directly contradicting Section 18(a) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as evidenced by the statement in Scraped Law Snippet 1: "Any person who shall make or cause to be made any statement in any application, report, or document filed pursuant to this chapter or any rule or regulation thereunder...which statement was at the time and in the light of the circumstances under which it was made false or misleading with respect to any material fact, shall be liable to any person who..." The change eliminates the 'good faith' defense, a critical element of compliance under securities law.

By: /s/ Thomas Sipp Thomas Sipp Intern SCULPTOR MASTER FUND LTD By: /s/ Thomas Sipp Thomas Sipp Intern
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the signatory's title to 'Intern' undermines the authority of the signatory, potentially invalidating the filing based on lack of proper authorization and misrepresentation of the signatory's role.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 6(a) requires registration statements to be signed by specified officers. The use of an 'Intern' as the signatory might contradict this requirement, as an intern may lack the proper authority to bind the company, therefore rendering the document as improperly signed and filed.

Registration of securities (a) Method of registration Any security may be registered with the Commission under the terms and conditions hereinafter provided, by filing a registration statement in triplicate, at least one of which shall be signed by each issuer, its principal executive officer or officers, its principal financial officer, its comptroller or principal accounting officer, an
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Chief Financial Officer' to 'Intern' directly contradicts the requirement in 15 USC 77f(a) that registration statements be signed by specific officers, including the principal financial officer. An 'Intern' lacks the authority and responsibility expected of a principal financial officer, thus making the document improperly signed, and creating a clear contradiction.
